Recaps/2026-W11-Recap.title: 2026-W11-Recap
Recaps/2026-W11-Recap.content:
Summary
This week focused on exploring nonergodic combinations of MESS3 processes and setting up infrastructure for running training experiments more efficiently. Made progress on visualizer tooling and began thinking more carefully about how to characterize distinguishability between process parameterizations.
Key Progress

Trained a transformer on fixed x but varying \alpha to study how parameterization affects learned representations
Added Voronoi-style visualization to the experiment visualizer
Built a Claude skill for setting up and running training experiments, which should speed up the iteration cycle going forward

Ideas and Questions

What does it mean for one MESS3 parameterization to be distinguishable from another? How does distinguishability change over context length?
For a given N number of MESS3s and context length L, what are the most distinguishable N parameterizations? At what N do components become indistinguishable to the Transformer?
Can we ask the network what parameterization it thinks it’s in — e.g., by training a classifier on different rollouts?
What does computational mechanics have to say about non-stationary processes?
What is the belief geometry of a POMDP?

Readings

Continued review of “From sequences to schemas” (ICML submission) — detailed notes on strengths, weaknesses, and open questions about their claims regarding low-rank recurrent dynamics and abstract relational representations.

Next Week

Design and run distinguishability experiments for nonergodic MESS3 combinations
Explore training on sequences with gaps of decreasing size
Finish alternating process write-up

References
